---
title: "Colorful PDF Demo"
subtitle: "Testing WeasyPrint vs Typst"
author: "SisengAI"
date: today
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    embed-resources: true
    css: demo.css
    toc: false
  typst:
    papersize: a4
    margin:
      x: 2.5cm
      y: 2cm
---

## What this is

A single `.qmd` file rendered through two pipelines:
- **WeasyPrint**: HTML → CSS → PDF
- **Typst**: Markdown → Typst → PDF

Both avoid LaTeX entirely.

## Colored box (CSS → WeasyPrint only)

::: {.amber-box}
This is an amber-colored box with a gradient background. In WeasyPrint it will look gorgeous. In Typst it falls back to a plain div.
:::

## Typst-native colored block

::: {.block fill="rgb(50, 18, 8)" inset="14pt" radius="6pt"}

[**Dark mahogany block**]{style="color: #FDFBF7"}

This block uses Typst's native `block()` function via the `.block` class. Works in both routes but designed for Typst.

:::

## Inline color

Here is [amber inline text]{style="color: #D4720A; font-weight: bold"} and here is [text with a blue background]{style="background-color: #2563EB; color: white; padding: 2pt"}.

## Code block

```python
def greet(name: str) -> str:
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

print(greet("SisengAI"))
```

## A table

| Method     | Color Power | Setup    |
| ---------- | ----------- | -------- |
| WeasyPrint | Full CSS    | Easy     |
| Typst      | Good        | Moderate |

## Final note

Render with:

```bash
# WeasyPrint
quarto render demo.qmd --to html && weasyprint demo.html ~/Documents/pdf-outputs/demo-weasy.pdf

# Typst
quarto render demo.qmd --to typst -o ~/Documents/pdf-outputs/demo-typst.pdf
```

Then compare the two PDFs side by side.
